title: Why Is My Cat Walking in Circles? Causes and Veterinary Guidance
meta_desc: Learn why your cat is walking in tight circles. Understand the causes, the medium urgency level, and how capturing video can help your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Circling in cats is an involuntary sign of a neurological or vestibular issue that requires a veterinary check-up within 24 hours. If your cat also shows symptoms like seizures or a head tilt, seek emergency care immediately.

What is circling behavior in cats and what does it mean?

Circling in cats occurs when a feline repeatedly walks in a tight, repetitive loop, often favoring one specific direction. Unlike normal play, this behavior is usually involuntary and serves as a clinical sign of a neurological or vestibular issue. It indicates that the cat's brain or inner ear is not properly processing its position in space.

Is my cat walking in circles a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for circling behavior is Medium; it is not a behavior that should be ignored or monitored at home for long.
  • You should contact a veterinarian for an appointment within 24 hours.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if the circling is accompanied by a head tilt, rhythmic eye flicking, seizures, or a total loss of appetite.

How can a video of my cat circling help the veterinarian?

  • Cats often hide symptoms when stressed at a veterinary clinic, so home footage is highly valuable.
  • Recording a short video of your cat walking in circles in their home environment helps the vet see the exact pattern of movement.
  • A video can reveal the direction of the circles and any subtle balance issues that might not be visible during a physical exam.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Compulsive circling in cats can be associated with various underlying medical conditions. Medical problems, including neuropathic pain, dermatologic conditions (such as adverse food reactions, atopic dermatitis, and parasitic hypersensitivity), myopathies, and focal seizures, must be excluded as potential causes. Neurologic causes include chronic lesions in the cerebral cortex and diencephalon, which may result in compulsive circling despite a relatively normal gait. Lesions of the midbrain, pons, and medulla oblongata can cause paresis or paralysis, while cerebellar lesions result in ataxia and dysmetria. Vestibular dysfunction can cause ipsilateral falling, rolling, or circling. A therapeutic response trial for neuropathic pain, seizures, pruritus, or compulsive disorders may be considered as part of the diagnostic process.

Chapter: Behavioral Medicine, Dermatology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1574)

faqs.question: Could an ear infection cause my cat to walk in circles?
faqs.answer: Yes, a deep inner ear infection can affect a cat's vestibular system, which controls balance, leading to circling and leaning to one side.

faqs.question: Is circling a sign of old age in cats?
faqs.answer: While senior cats may develop cognitive dysfunction, sudden circling is more often related to medical issues like high blood pressure, strokes, or tumors rather than just normal aging.

faqs.question: What should I do while waiting for the vet appointment?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat in a confined, safe area with no stairs or sharp corners to prevent them from falling or injuring themselves while their balance is compromised.
